narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass without needing retakes. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of 5.53, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to most students who ranked much higher. This low ranking in your later years outweighed other strengths, pushing the prediction firmly toward failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest influence, pulling the prediction toward success because it demonstrated strong academic ability beyond the typical applicant. Similarly, your LSAT score of 34—though below the average 36.70—still contributed positively by showing decent standardized test performance relative to what the model expects. Other details, like your lower-middle family income bracket and part-time enrollment plans (uncommon, as 93% of students go full-time), played smaller roles but didn't shift the balance enough. Compared to typical students, your early Year 1 decile of 3 was also below average at 5.71, hinting at a pattern of underperformance that amplified concerns. While your profile shows promise in college-level work, the model saw your school grades, especially in Year 3, as the biggest red flag for bar success. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, aiming for rankings closer to 5 or higher, and perhaps retake the LSAT to push past 36. Building a stronger recent academic record could flip this prediction in your favor for future applications. You're clearly capable based on your UGPA, so targeted improvements here might open doors at other schools too. Keep pushing—many applicants turn things around with focused effort.
